title: Reading and Use of English – Part 8
description: You are going to read an article about four people who joined an archaeological dig as volunteers. For questions 47–56, choose from the people A–D. The people may be chosen more than once.

texts.D:
### Marco, The Roman Villa Project
'I expected to find gold, but I found a new respect for mud,' Marco laughs. [51]The most difficult part was the painstaking pace; I spent three days clearing a single square meter with a toothbrush.[/51] [48]I was surprised to find that the constant squatting and reaching significantly improved my flexibility.[/48] [53]I realized that history isn't just about dates; it's about the tangible objects people held in their hands every day.[/53] [50]I found that my background in technical drawing was incredibly useful for documenting the site's layout accurately.[/50] [47]Initially, the lack of internet and basic facilities was a shock, but I grew to appreciate the simplicity.[/47] It was a truly grounding experience.
texts.C:
### Sarah, The Neolithic Settlement
Sarah traveled to a remote island for the dig. 'The isolation was the main attraction,' she says. [47]I hadn't expected the physical toll of the wind and rain to affect my morale so much in the first week.[/47] [54]What kept me going was the infectious enthusiasm of the lead archaeologist, who could make a piece of charcoal sound fascinating.[/54] [52]I hadn't anticipated the emotional weight of finding a child's footprint preserved in the clay; it felt very personal.[/52] [49]The experience taught me that our modern problems are fleeting when viewed against the scale of millennia.[/49] [56]If I hadn't won a community heritage grant, I never could have afforded the travel costs.[/56] It was a transformative journey.
texts.B:
### Victor, The Underwater Dig
Victor helped excavate a shipwreck. 'It was like working in slow motion,' he notes. [51]We struggled with the limited visibility and the cold water, which made even simple tasks incredibly complex.[/51] [48]I noticed that my swimming stamina reached an all-time high, which was a side effect I hadn't planned for.[/48] [53]I realized that archaeology is as much about patience and teamwork as it is about discovery.[/53] [50]I found that the precision I used in my previous career as a watchmaker was perfect for handling fragile artifacts.[/50] [55]It took a while to adjust to the rhythm of the tides, which dictated our entire working day.[/55] [52]I’d initially worried that it would be too claustrophobic, but I felt surprisingly free.
texts.A:
### Elena, The Medieval Monastery
Elena volunteered at a site in a busy city. 'History was literally under the pavement,' she says. [49]The project made me realize that our cities are living museums with layers of stories waiting to be told.[/49] [56]I wouldn't have found the project if my history professor hadn't recommended it as a practical elective.[/56] [54]I was moved by the camaraderie among the diverse group of volunteers, who ranged from students to retirees.[/54] [47]I hadn't expected the noise of the city to be so distracting while trying to focus on such delicate work.[/47] [55]I found the initial weeks of sorting through repetitive pottery shards quite tedious and frustrating.[/55] [52]I’d been concerned that I’d be too clumsy, but I learned to be incredibly gentle.

questions.question: mentions being surprised by how the environment affected their mental state or focus?
questions.explanation: Marco (A) mentions the shock of no facilities, Sarah (B) mentions wind/rain affecting morale, and Elena (D) mentions city noise distracting her.

questions.question: notes a positive physical change that resulted from the manual labor?
questions.explanation: Marco (A) mentions improved flexibility, and Victor (C) mentions his swimming stamina.

questions.question: expresses a change in their perception of time or the environment?
questions.explanation: Sarah (B) sees problems as fleeting against millennia, and Elena (D) sees cities as living museums.

questions.question: mentions how their professional background or skills were advantageous to the project?
questions.explanation: Marco (A) used technical drawing, and Victor (C) used his watchmaking precision.

questions.question: describes a specific technical or physical difficulty that slowed down the work?
questions.explanation: Marco (A) mentions the toothbrush pace, and Victor (C) mentions limited visibility and cold water.

questions.question: admits to having had prior fears or having a strong emotional reaction to a find?
questions.explanation: Sarah (B) felt the emotional weight of a footprint, Victor (C) worried about claustrophobia, and Elena (D) about being clumsy.

questions.question: reflects on a deeper philosophical lesson learned about the nature of the work?
questions.explanation: Marco (A) realized history is about objects, and Victor (C) about patience and teamwork.

questions.question: highlights the value of the people they worked with during the dig?
questions.explanation: Sarah (B) mentions the lead archaeologist's enthusiasm, and Elena (D) the camaraderie among volunteers.

questions.question: describes a period of adjustment or frustration with the routine?
questions.explanation: Victor (C) adjusted to the tides, and Elena (D) found sorting pottery shards tedious at first.

questions.question: acknowledges an external factor or person that enabled their participation?
questions.explanation: Sarah (B) mentions a heritage grant, and Elena (D) mentions her professor's recommendation.
